The surgical team places a 15mm trocar in the left midclavicular line and a 5mm trocar in the epigastrium, using a laparoscopic approach with a 10mm 30-degree scope under 12mmHg pressure.
The gravid uterus is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon performs desufflation of the left flank to facilitate the insertion of a 5mm trocar by the assistant.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the attachments of the lower pole of the spleen, including the colosplenic ligament, while contracting the spleen with a grasper. The phrenocolic and phrenosplenic ligaments are also divided, freeing the spleen from the ventral surface of the left kidney.

The spleen is mobilized, and a four by four gauze is introduced to provide anterior retraction of the left lobe of the liver. The gastro-splenic ligament is exposed, and the assistant provides lateral retraction of the spleen. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the choro-gastric vessels, freeing the spleen from its attachments.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery and begins dissection using a harmonic scalpel, progressing proximally with a hook instrument. The hook is used to carefully isolate the splenic artery from the tail of the pancreas, with the artery clearly visible as the spleen is isolated.
A small accessory splenic artery is identified, clipped, and divided with scissors. A window is then created using the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon lifts the spleen using two graspers, exposing the splenic vein. The aneurysm is visible above the spleen.
